schools.summary: The anger was kindled not by the census but by the pride behind it; 'he moved' is God attributing to himself what he permits, and the verb here means simply 'to count.'
Matthew Henry weighs six theories of the sin and settles on pride of heart; God permits what Satan suggests, taking occasion to punish Israel.
Gill hears an echo of the earlier famine for Saul's guilt; the wrath breaks out afresh for secret sin or the late rebellions.
Clarke: David, slackening in piety and eyeing an extension of empire, is provoked by Satan to count his fighting men.

debates.question: Who 'moved' David to number Israel — the Lord (2 Samuel 24:1) or Satan (1 Chronicles 21:1)?

sides.position: God moved him by permission, having ordained that such a sin should be the natural fruit of such a proud state
Satan was the tempter; God as righteous Judge only permitted it

schools.summary: 'From Dan to Beersheba' marks the whole land; the verb is a military muster (Numbers 1:44), and the reading 'who was with him' is defended against the Chronicler's variant.
Gill: Joab, now a counsellor as well as general, is the man placed to carry the commission through the kingdom.
Even the irreligious Joab saw the act as abominable and against the national principle; his objection, sustained by the captains, was overridden by sheer self-will.
Clarke: 'this very bad man' argued sensibly against a ruinous measure, but the infatuated king would hear no reason.
Gill: Joab now protests respectfully, unlike his harsh rebuke over Absalom's mourning.
Gill: the captains agreed with Joab, yet the king's determined command bound them to execute what they had condemned.
Keil and Delitzsch trace the muster's start at Aroer of Gad, distinct from Aroer on the Arnon, the surveyors encamping in the open country.
Ellicott lays out the census route beginning east of Jordan at the far south; Aroer is on the Arnon; the text after it is judged corrupt beyond recovery.
Gill: the crossing of Jordan was to number Reuben, Gad, and half-Manasseh first.
Clarke maps the northern names: Tahtim-hodshi a newly inhabited land, Dan-jaan 'Dan of the woods,' the northmost city as Beersheba is the southmost.
Ellicott judges 'Tahtim-hodshi' a corruption (perhaps 'Hittites'); Zidon and Tyre were never truly possessed by Israel.
Gill: Gilead, split among Reuben, Gad, and half-Manasseh, was numbered first of all.
The circuit turns south through the Hivite and Canaanite towns of later Galilee to Beersheba; the totals (800,000/500,000) diverge from Chronicles as round approximations.
Gill: 'the strong hold of Tyre' means old Tyre and its parts, since the island city lay outside Israel and was not to be numbered.
The nine-months-and-twenty-days span frames the numbers as round approximations resting on oral tradition, never entered in the annals (1 Chronicles 27:24).
Clarke stresses the labour, comparing it to William the Conqueror's six-year Domesday survey, and judging Joab's task the harder.
Gill traces the circuit east, north, west, then south, which finished it.
Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch explain the gap with Chronicles by Joab's unwilling, incomplete count (Levi and Benjamin omitted) and differing inclusions, not textual error.
Clarke frankly ascribes the discrepancy to scribal corruption, distinguishing the inspired writers from fallible copyists.
Gill moves past the total to note that once the sin was done 'David's heart smote him.'

debates.question: Why do the census totals differ from 1 Chronicles 21:5?

sides.position: A copyist's corruption; both figures cannot be right
The natural residue of an incomplete, unofficial count with different inclusions

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: conscience woke the very night the count was finished; true penitents see the evil others miss, owning they have done 'very foolishly' in pride.
Gill anatomizes the fall — pride, confidence in the creature, no seeking of God, no half-shekel ransom (Exodus 30:12), perhaps a poll-tax intended.
Ellicott: unlike after Bathsheba, David's conscience woke without a prophet — though only after ten months had passed.
Clarke: David likely coveted an extension of empire; when God is offended, vain is the help of man.
Ellicott: read 'and when'; the prophet was sent because David had already repented, not the reverse; Gad may have penned this account (1 Chronicles 29:29).
Gill: render 'and David arose'; his conviction and confession came in the night, before Gad was sent.
Clarke supposes the contrition arose from Gad's reproof, the reproof preceding the confession of v.10.

debates.question: Did Gad's reproof produce David's repentance, or follow it?

sides.position: The repentance came first, in the night; the prophet was sent in response to it
The reproof came first and produced the contrition

schools.summary: Gill: the dropped title — plain 'David,' not 'my servant David' (2 Samuel 7:5) — marks a servant under correction.
Keil and Delitzsch frame vv.11-14 as one period: God offers three judgments that David may choose, the doing kept in God's hand.
Ellicott: 'three years' (not seven) suits the symmetry of three months and three days, as Chronicles and the Septuagint read.
Clarke: 'seven' is a copyist's error for 'three,' the Hebrew letters being easily confused (1 Chronicles 21:12).
Gill: Gad speaks only of correction, not sin, confirming David was already convicted.

sides.position: Three years — the true reading, matching Chronicles, the Septuagint, and the threefold pattern

schools.summary: Clarke: a noble choice — war and famine would have spared David himself, but the pestilence exposed him equally with the meanest subject.
Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch: David's earlier spirit reappears, choosing the judgment most directly from God's hand and least from man's, and granting himself no immunity.
Gill: David does not name his choice, but by the event he chose the pestilence.

themes: falling into the hand of the Lord
a choice that spares him nothing

schools.summary: The disputed phrase 'the time appointed' is read either as three days or, more probably, as 'the time of assembly' (evening sacrifice), shrinking the plague to a single day; the angel enters the record here.
Clarke reads 'the time appointed' of the three days, but notes the plague ended before the third day closed, sparing Jerusalem.
Gill: the Lord directly employed an angel, empowering him to spread a pestilential disease.

debates.question: How long did the pestilence last — 'the time appointed'?

sides.position: Three days, though stayed before the third closed
A single day, to 'the time of assembly' (the evening sacrifice)

schools.summary: 'Jehovah repented of the evil' means he stayed the angel's hand and spared Jerusalem, not that he changed his mind (Exodus 32:14; Genesis 6:6); the angel was visibly present at Araunah's floor.

Matthew Henry, with Lightfoot: on the very Moriah where Abraham was stayed from slaying Isaac, the angel is stayed from destroying Jerusalem — for the sake of the great Sacrifice our lives are spared.
Gill: the angel stood between earth and heaven with a drawn sword over the city, a menace to strike terror.
Clarke: the angel was truly an object of sight, seen by David, Ornan, and his sons (1 Chronicles 21:20).
Keil and Delitzsch: true penitential prayer takes all blame on self; David, the guilty shepherd, pleads the flock innocent of this particular sin.
Gill: David prayed to Jehovah, not the angel, he and the elders in sackcloth fallen on their faces.
Clarke: kings can be punished in their office only through their land; David offers his own house to bear the stroke.
Matthew Henry: the command to build an altar proves God reconciled; peace is made only by sacrifice, by Christ the great propitiation typified in all the offerings.
Keil and Delitzsch: the altar's offerings were to appease wrath and avert the plague; Ellicott adds that this event fixed the Temple-site on Moriah (1 Chronicles 21:18).
Clarke names Moriah as the traditional site of Abel's, Abraham's, and Solomon's altars.
Gill: Gad came ordered by the angel of the Lord.
Gill: David went up from the lower city to Moriah, to the threshing-floor, a place for winnowing threshed corn.
Keil and Delitzsch: Araunah looked out and bowed, and David stated his errand plainly — to buy the floor and build an altar that the plague be turned away.
Keil and Delitzsch: Araunah looked out, went forth, and bowed to the earth; Ellicott notes the Hebrew here says he saw 'the king,' where Chronicles says 'the angel,' the words being much alike.
Gill: Araunah and his four sons had hidden for fear of the angel (1 Chronicles 21:20), and he now peeps out.

themes: the terror still on the floor

schools.summary: Gill: Araunah marvels that so great a king should visit a subject's threshing-floor — a condescension he wonders at.
Keil and Delitzsch give David's bare answer: to buy the floor, build an altar, and stay the plague from the people.

themes: the people's good, not the king's greatness

schools.summary: Clarke: Araunah's one gift springs from love to king, people, his own safety, and the honour of God, that God might magnify justice and mercy.
Ellicott: Araunah does not hesitate, considers the altar-site settled, and would gladly have made the whole a gift.
Gill: he offers ground, oxen, and wooden implements for fuel — whatever was fit for the service.

themes: the coming principle of costly sacrifice

schools.summary: Gill takes 'as a king' of Araunah's royal Jebusite blood or princely liberality, citing the Ulysses-Antinous parallel.
Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch read the clause as Araunah's own speech; David refuses the gift, buying floor and oxen for fifty shekels of silver (vs. Chronicles' 600 gold).
Clarke: there was no king of the Jebusites; 'the king' is likely a scribal intrusion, absent from the Septuagint, versions, and Chronicles.

sides.position: Yes — a token of his royal descent or princely generosity
No — 'the king' is a scribal intrusion; render the clause as Araunah's own words

themes: the price refused as a gift
silver and gold in the two accounts

schools.summary: Clarke's maxim: a religion that costs nothing is worth nothing; Araunah's gift would have been Araunah's sacrifice, not David's.
Ellicott: the fifty shekels of silver (covering ground and oxen) differ from Chronicles' 600 shekels of gold; one figure was altered in transcription.
Gill: David insisted on the full price, taking no advantage of Araunah's liberality.

debates.question: Fifty shekels of silver, or six hundred of gold (1 Chronicles 21:25)?

sides.position: The difference is a transcriptional alteration; the gold/silver harmonization fails
Corruption crept into the numbers of the historical books

themes: the price in the two accounts

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott: burnt- and peace-offerings close the book; fire from heaven sealed the acceptance, and David fixed this floor as the Temple-site (1 Chronicles 22:1).
Clarke: the fire from heaven (1 Chronicles 21:26) proved the sacrifice pleasing; Jerusalem was spared, and the book is left unfinished.
Matthew Henry: Christ is our altar and sacrifice, in whom alone we escape the flaming sword and find favour with God.
Gill: the altar rose after the purchase, the sacrifice following the price paid.

themes: the altar and the stayed plague
